summary: Gruber analyzes how the MacBook Air disrupts Apple's traditionally clean product lineup hierarchy. He notes that Apple usually structures its lines with a clear default choice, a high-end option, and a budget option, as seen with iPods and desktop Macs. The Air complicates the MacBook lineup by creating two different $999 options with different tradeoffs. He argues the Air is best understood as a secondary Mac rather than a primary computer, due to its storage and port limitations. This framing leads him to conclude that the Air, especially the 11-inch model, competes more with the iPad than with other MacBooks, occupying a 'pro' version of the same in-between market segment.
key_points: Apple typically structures product lines to eliminate 'which one should I buy?' ambiguity, with a clear default, high-end, and budget option
The MacBook Air breaks this pattern by creating a parallel upgrade path (lighter/thinner) alongside the MacBook Pro (more powerful)
The Air is best understood as a secondary Mac for people who already have a primary desktop or laptop
The 11-inch Air competes more with the iPad than with other MacBooks, serving as a 'pro' solution for the same in-between market segment
Storage limitations are the Air's biggest weakness, but matter less for a secondary machine
money_quotes: Apple goes to great effort to eliminate 'which one should I buy?' ambiguity from its product lines.
The MacBook Air defies this pattern.

If you start with the $999 13-inch MacBook as the baseline model, there are two entirely different paths to spend more: go for performance with a MacBook Pro, or go lighter and thinner with a 13-inch Air.
The Air is a secondary Mac; MacBook Pros are for use as a primary computer.
The new MacBook Airs — particularly the 11-inch model — don't compete against the other MacBooks so much as they do the iPad.
It's like a 'pro' solution for the same 'in between a smartphone and a full-size laptop PC' market segment that the iPad sits in.
key_insight: The MacBook Air is best understood not as a laptop competitor but as a pro-grade alternative to the iPad, serving the same in-between market segment with more capability.
